steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researchers' hypothesis is that steel is more suitable than concrete for a tall building's lower floors. To support this, we need a finding where the steel-lower-floor building performs better than the concrete one under wind and gravity over a century.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — During the simulation, the structure with steel lower floors bent in high wind to a greater degree than did the structure with concrete lower floors.: Greater bending means steel performed worse under wind, which contradicts the hypothesis that steel is more suitable. ✗
  • Choice B — At the end of the simulation, the structure with concrete lower floors was several inches shorter than the structure with steel lower floors.: If the concrete building shrank while the steel one did not, the concrete lower floors compressed more under the weight of the upper floors. Steel resisted the load better, supporting the hypothesis that it is more suitable. ✓
  • Choice C — During the simulation, when sudden forces caused vibrations in the structures, those vibrations took longer to disappear in the structure with steel lower floors than in the structure with concrete lower floors.: Longer-lasting vibrations in the steel building means steel dampens dynamic forces less effectively — a disadvantage, not a demonstration of superiority. ✗
  • Choice D — At the end of the simulation, the structure with steel lower floors had slightly larger dimensions at its base than it did at the beginning of the simulation.: Expansion at the base could indicate deformation or instability in the steel structure, which does not support a claim that steel is more suitable. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
